title: Migrate Postgres connection string from ConfigMap to Kubernetes Secret
description: The Postgres connection string (CNPG_SIGMA1_PG_URL) is currently stored in a plaintext ConfigMap, which is not encrypted at rest and is accessible to anyone with read access to ConfigMaps in the namespace. To mitigate this, migrate the Postgres connection string to a Kubernetes Secret. Update all consumers (PM server, smoke test pod, etc.) to reference the Secret via envFrom or secretRef. The ConfigMap should only contain non-sensitive values.
subtasks: Create a Kubernetes Secret to store the Postgres connection string (CNPG_SIGMA1_PG_URL), sourcing the value from the CNPG-generated app secret.
Update the PM server and smoke test pod manifests to mount the Postgres connection string from the new Secret using envFrom or secretRef.
Remove the Postgres connection string from the sigma-1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap.
Update documentation and deployment scripts to reflect the new Secret-based approach.
Test: Deploy the updated manifests and verify that the PM server and smoke test pod can still connect to Postgres using the environment variable from the Secret.
Run OWASP ZAP scan against the PM server endpoints to ensure no sensitive data is exposed in environment variables or logs.
acceptance_criteria: The Postgres connection string is no longer present in any ConfigMap.
All consumers successfully read the connection string from the Kubernetes Secret.
Access to the Secret is restricted to only the necessary ServiceAccounts.
Smoke test pod and PM server pass connectivity checks to Postgres.
OWASP ZAP scan shows no leakage of database credentials.

title: Enforce authentication and authorization on all PM server API endpoints
description: PM server API endpoints currently lack explicit authentication and authorization, and use a single shared static SERVICE_API_KEY for all service-to-service authentication. Implement middleware-level authentication for all endpoints, require the SERVICE_API_KEY in the Authorization header, and consider moving to per-service API keys to reduce blast radius. Ensure bridge services can handle key rotation and accept old/new keys during transitions.
subtasks: Implement authentication middleware in the Elysia router that validates the Authorization header for all API endpoints.
Require the SERVICE_API_KEY for all API calls; reject requests without a valid key with a 401 response.
Refactor the authentication logic to support per-service API keys (e.g., DISCORD_SERVICE_KEY, LINEAR_SERVICE_KEY) and update bridge services to validate the correct key.
Update secret management and rotation logic to support multiple keys and ensure bridge services can accept both old and new keys during rotation.
Add explicit tests to verify that unauthenticated requests receive 401 responses and that key rotation does not disrupt service.
Document the authentication and key rotation process for all internal services.
Run OWASP ZAP scan to verify all endpoints require authentication.
acceptance_criteria: All PM server API endpoints require a valid Authorization header with the correct API key.
Per-service API keys are supported and enforced for bridge services.
Key rotation can be performed without downtime; both old and new keys are accepted during the transition window.
Unauthenticated requests consistently receive 401 responses.
OWASP ZAP scan confirms no unauthenticated endpoints are accessible.

title: Implement input validation and sanitization for pipeline trigger PRD payload
description: The pipeline trigger endpoint (POST /api/pipeline/trigger) does not validate or sanitize the PRD payload, which could lead to injection attacks or hash collision exploits. Implement strict input validation, enforce schema and size limits, and sanitize all user-supplied text before embedding it in downstream systems (Linear, GitHub, idempotency keys).
subtasks: Define a JSON schema for the PRD payload and enforce it at the API boundary using a validation library.
Set maximum size limits for the PRD payload and all user-supplied fields.
Sanitize all user-supplied text to prevent injection into Linear issue descriptions, GitHub PR bodies, and idempotency key comments.
Ensure the idempotency key HTML comment cannot be spoofed by escaping or filtering out dangerous sequences (e.g., '-->').
Add automated tests for malformed, oversized, and malicious payloads to verify rejection or proper sanitization.
Run OWASP ZAP scan and static analysis to verify no injection vectors remain.
acceptance_criteria: PRD payloads are validated against a strict schema and size limits.
All user-supplied text is sanitized before being used in downstream systems.
Idempotency key comments cannot be spoofed or injected by PRD content.
Malicious or malformed payloads are rejected with clear error messages.
OWASP ZAP scan and static analysis show no injection vulnerabilities.

title: Restrict API token scopes and validate permissions for Linear and GitHub integrations
description: The Linear and GitHub API tokens used by the PM server are not documented or validated for minimum required scopes. Overly permissive tokens could be exploited if compromised. Document and enforce the minimum required scopes for each token, and add a validation step to ensure tokens do not have excessive permissions.
subtasks: Document the minimum required scopes for the GitHub and Linear API tokens (e.g., repo, contents:write, pull_requests:write for GitHub; issue creation for Linear).
Update the secret provisioning and onboarding documentation to require only the documented scopes.
Implement a startup or smoke test that queries the token permissions and verifies they match the expected scopes.
Add automated tests to ensure tokens with excessive permissions are rejected or flagged.
Update the onboarding checklist to include scope validation for all new tokens.
Run a review of all existing tokens and rotate any that are overly permissive.
acceptance_criteria: Documentation clearly specifies the minimum required scopes for each API token.
Startup or smoke test fails if a token has excessive permissions.
All tokens in use are verified to have only the required scopes.
No tokens with admin or destructive permissions are used.
Acceptance tests pass with tokens at minimum required scope.

title: Implement log sanitization and sensitive data redaction in structured logs
description: Structured logs may leak sensitive data such as API tokens, webhook URLs, or PII. Add a log sanitization layer to redact known sensitive patterns and establish a logging policy to truncate and sanitize error messages from external APIs. Never log full HTTP request/response bodies for external API calls.
subtasks: Implement a log sanitization middleware in the logger module that redacts API keys, Bearer tokens, webhook URLs, and other sensitive patterns from all log entries.
Update all logging calls to use the sanitization layer before output.
Truncate and sanitize error messages from external APIs before logging.
Audit all existing log statements for potential sensitive data leakage.
Add automated tests to verify that sensitive data is never present in logs.
Run a log review on recent logs to confirm no sensitive data is present.
acceptance_criteria: All logs are passed through the sanitization layer before output.
No API keys, tokens, or webhook URLs appear in any log entries.
Error messages from external APIs are truncated and sanitized.
Automated tests confirm that sensitive data is redacted.
Log review finds no sensitive data in recent logs.

title: Configure Redis authentication and update connection strings
description: Redis is currently deployed without authentication, allowing any pod in the namespace to access all data. Configure Redis with a password stored in a Kubernetes Secret, update the connection string to include the password, and ensure Sentinel (if used) also uses authentication.
subtasks: Generate a strong Redis password and store it in a Kubernetes Secret via ExternalSecrets.
Update the Redis Deployment/StatefulSet to set the requirepass configuration using the Secret.
Update the Redis connection string in all consumers (ConfigMap or Secret) to include the password.
If using Redis Sentinel, configure sentinel auth-pass to use the same password.
Test: Deploy the updated configuration and verify that unauthenticated connections are rejected and authenticated connections succeed.
Run a security scan to verify Redis is not accessible without authentication.
acceptance_criteria: Redis requires authentication for all connections.
All consumers use the updated connection string with the password.
Sentinel (if used) is configured with auth-pass.
Unauthenticated connections are rejected.
Security scan confirms Redis is not accessible without authentication.

title: Enforce Pod Security Standards and explicit securityContext on all pods
description: No Pod Security Standards (PSS) are enforced in the namespace, allowing containers to run as root or with excessive privileges. Apply Pod Security Admission labels and add explicit securityContext to all pod specs to enforce least privilege.
subtasks: Apply Pod Security Admission labels to the namespace (e.g., pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted).
Update all pod specs (PM server, Redis, smoke test) to include securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities: {drop: ['ALL']}.
Assign specific non-root UIDs for Redis and smoke test containers.
Audit all existing deployments for missing or insecure securityContext fields.
Test: Attempt to deploy a pod that violates the PSS and verify it is rejected.
Run a security scan to confirm no pods are running as root or with privileged access.
acceptance_criteria: Namespace has Pod Security Admission labels enforcing at least 'restricted' policy.
All pods have explicit securityContext fields set to least privilege.
Pods running as root or with privileged access are rejected.
Security scan confirms all pods comply with Pod Security Standards.

title: Implement alerting for security-relevant events in the cluster
description: There is no alerting configured for security-relevant events such as repeated authentication failures, circuit breaker state changes, secret rotation failures, or network policy violations. Configure alerting rules using Prometheus AlertManager or the cluster's alerting system to notify the operations team via Discord webhook.
subtasks: Define alerting rules for ExternalSecret sync failures, repeated 401/403 responses from bridge services, Cilium policy deny events, and circuit breaker transitions to 'open'.
Configure Prometheus AlertManager (or equivalent) to send notifications to the existing Discord webhook.
Integrate alerting with the cluster's monitoring stack and ensure all relevant metrics are scraped.
Test: Simulate each alert condition and verify that an alert is sent to the Discord channel.
Document the alerting rules and notification process.
acceptance_criteria: Alerting rules are in place for all specified security-relevant events.
Alerts are sent to the operations team via Discord webhook.
Simulated alert conditions trigger notifications as expected.
Documentation exists for alerting rules and notification flow.
